words.definition: difficult to understand; obscure
words.example: The philosopher's abstruse arguments baffled even his colleagues.

words.definition: belonging to a period other than the one being portrayed; outdated
words.example: The film's anachronistic costumes distracted from its historical accuracy.

words.definition: of doubtful authenticity, although widely circulated as true
words.example: The story of the falling apple is likely apocryphal.

words.definition: an abrupt transition from the elevated to the ordinary, producing an anticlimax
words.example: The tragic scene collapsed into bathos with one clumsy line.

words.definition: high-sounding but with little meaning; inflated
words.example: The senator's bombastic speech impressed no one with substance.

words.definition: excessively complicated, typically involving intrigue
words.example: Navigating the byzantine bureaucracy took months.

words.definition: the treatment of light and shade in art; a strong contrast
words.example: Caravaggio is renowned for his dramatic chiaroscuro.

words.definition: having knowledge or awareness of something
words.example: The author was cognizant of the criticism her work would receive.

words.definition: stubbornly disobedient; resistant to authority
words.example: The contumacious witness refused to answer the court's questions.

words.definition: causing harm, often in a subtle or unexpected way
words.example: Excessive screen time can have a deleterious effect on sleep.

words.definition: a political leader who exploits popular prejudices for personal gain
words.example: Historians warn against the rise of demagogues in times of crisis.

words.definition: the final resolution of the plot of a narrative
words.example: The novel's denouement ties every subplot together.

words.definition: light, delicate, and translucent
words.example: The dancer wore a diaphanous veil that caught the stage light.

words.definition: cheerful and full of energy
words.example: Her ebullient personality lit up every room she entered.

words.definition: relating to the Christian Church or its clergy
words.example: The novel is set within an ecclesiastical court of the medieval era.

words.definition: affected, overrefined, and lacking vigour
words.example: The critic dismissed the poetry as effete and self-indulgent.

words.definition: expressing sorrowful lamentation, especially for the dead
words.example: The poem's elegiac tone mourns a vanished world.

words.definition: the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature and limits of knowledge
words.example: Her thesis draws heavily on Cartesian epistemology.

words.definition: mental calmness and composure, especially in difficulty
words.example: He accepted the harsh review with remarkable equanimity.

words.definition: having or showing great knowledge or learning
words.example: The erudite professor could discuss any era of history with ease.

words.definition: extremely delicate and light in a way that seems too perfect for this world
words.example: The choir's ethereal harmonies filled the cathedral.

words.definition: the study of the origin and historical development of words
words.example: The etymology of 'quarantine' traces back to the Italian for forty days.

words.definition: the quality of being pleasing to the ear
words.example: The poet chose each word for its euphony as much as its meaning.

words.definition: relating to human existence, especially its meaning and purpose
words.example: The novel raises existential questions about free will.

words.definition: producing or capable of producing abundant results; fertile
words.example: The Renaissance was a fecund period for the arts.

words.definition: well chosen or apt; pleasingly appropriate
words.example: She found a felicitous phrase to close her speech.

words.definition: excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters
words.example: The garrulous old sailor told the same story three times.

words.definition: the theory and methodology of interpretation, especially of texts
words.example: Biblical hermeneutics has shaped centuries of theological debate.

words.definition: excessive pride or self-confidence, often leading to downfall
words.example: The tragic hero's hubris ultimately causes his ruin.

words.definition: the rejection or destruction of cherished beliefs and institutions
words.example: The movement's iconoclasm shocked the art establishment.

words.definition: unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable
words.example: Old age is an ineluctable fact of life.

words.definition: just begun and not fully formed; rudimentary
words.example: Her inchoate plan needed far more detail before it could work.

words.definition: not able to be reformed or corrected
words.example: The incorrigible prankster was expelled after his fifth offence.

words.definition: too great or extreme to be expressed in words
words.example: She felt an ineffable joy watching the sunrise from the summit.

words.definition: showing a casual lack of concern; carefree
words.example: His insouciant attitude towards deadlines infuriated his editor.

words.definition: unwilling to change one's views or agree about something
words.example: The two sides remained intransigent throughout the negotiations.

words.definition: harsh, abusive language directed against someone
words.example: The pamphlet was full of invective against the ruling class.

words.definition: using very few words; terse
words.example: His laconic reply gave nothing away.

words.definition: looking or sounding sad and dismal
words.example: The lugubrious music set the tone for the film's final act.

words.definition: a plot or scheme, typically involving underhand means
words.example: The play centres on the machinations of a scheming courtier.

words.definition: generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival
words.example: The champion was magnanimous in victory, praising her opponent.

words.definition: sweet or musical; pleasant to hear
words.example: His mellifluous voice made even the shipping forecast sound beautiful.

words.definition: not telling the truth; lying
words.example: The report exposed the minister's mendacious claims.

words.definition: a figure of speech in which the name of an attribute is substituted for the thing itself
words.example: 'The crown' is a metonymy for the monarchy.

words.definition: imitation or representation of reality in art and literature
words.example: Aristotle considered mimesis central to the purpose of tragedy.

words.definition: a person who dislikes humankind and avoids human society
words.example: The old misanthrope lived alone at the edge of the village.

words.definition: vague, unclear, or ill-defined
words.example: The proposal remained nebulous even after the meeting.

words.definition: to deliberately make something unclear or confusing
words.example: The report seemed designed to obfuscate the true costs.

words.definition: obedient or attentive to an excessive, servile degree
words.example: The obsequious waiter fussed over every tiny detail.

words.definition: the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature of being and existence
words.example: The seminar examined questions of ontology raised by artificial intelligence.

words.definition: harsh public criticism or disgrace
words.example: The scandal brought widespread opprobrium upon the company.

words.definition: designed to impress or attract notice; showy
words.example: The billionaire's ostentatious mansion dominated the coastline.

words.definition: a public speech or text in praise of someone
words.example: The eulogy became a panegyric to the professor's decades of teaching.

words.definition: travelling from place to place, especially for work
words.example: The peripatetic scholar lectured at a dozen universities each year.

words.definition: having keen insight or understanding; shrewd
words.example: Her perspicacious analysis exposed the flaw in the argument.

words.definition: holding firmly to an opinion or course of action; stubbornly persistent
words.example: His pertinacious pursuit of the truth eventually solved the case.

words.definition: a person who is hostile or indifferent to culture and the arts
words.example: He was mocked as a philistine for never having read a novel.

words.definition: relating to an episodic style of fiction dealing with the adventures of a roguish hero
words.example: The novel follows a picaresque hero through eighteenth-century Europe.

words.definition: relating to the common people; unrefined
words.example: The senator sneered at what he called plebeian tastes.

words.definition: a strong verbal or written attack on a particular opinion
words.example: The pamphlet is a fierce polemic against censorship.

words.definition: lacking imagination; dull and ordinary
words.example: His prosaic description failed to capture the beauty of the scene.

words.definition: the place of origin or earliest known history of something
words.example: Experts questioned the painting's provenance before the auction.

words.definition: exceedingly idealistic; unrealistic and impractical
words.example: His quixotic scheme to end world hunger single-handedly impressed no investors.

words.definition: having an obstinately uncooperative attitude
words.example: The recalcitrant committee refused to approve any changes.

words.definition: little known; abstruse and obscure
words.example: The lecture delved into recondite corners of medieval law.

words.definition: strongly reminiscent or suggestive of something
words.example: The old house was redolent of a bygone era.

words.definition: to make an abstract idea concrete or real
words.example: The monument reifies the nation's collective memory of the war.

words.definition: grimly mocking or cynical
words.example: He gave a sardonic smile at the absurd suggestion.

words.definition: relating to tailoring, clothes, or style of dress
words.example: The actor was known as much for his sartorial elegance as his talent.

words.definition: showing dedication and diligence
words.example: Her sedulous research took nearly a decade to complete.

words.definition: the view that the self is all that can be known to exist
words.example: The novel's narrator descends into a kind of solipsism, doubting all else is real.

words.definition: the use of clever but false arguments, especially to deceive
words.example: Critics accused the debater of sophistry rather than genuine argument.

words.definition: the amalgamation of different religions, cultures, or schools of thought
words.example: The temple's architecture reflects a striking religious syncretism.

words.definition: saying the same thing twice in different words, needlessly
words.example: 'Free gift' is a common tautology in advertising.

words.definition: relating to the explanation of phenomena by their purpose rather than their cause
words.example: The essay offers a teleological reading of evolutionary theory.

words.definition: a lament for the dead; a song of mourning
words.example: The symphony's final movement is a threnody for the fallen.

words.definition: mentally or physically inactive; lethargic
words.example: The heat left the whole town feeling torpid by midafternoon.

words.definition: shaking or quivering slightly, often from fear or weakness
words.example: Her tremulous voice betrayed how nervous she was.

words.definition: eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant
words.example: The truculent customer refused to accept any apology.

words.definition: the fact of appearing everywhere at once
words.example: The ubiquity of smartphones has transformed daily life.

words.definition: offence or annoyance
words.example: She took umbrage at his careless remark.

words.definition: excessively flattering or ingratiating; oily in manner
words.example: The unctuous salesman's compliments made her suspicious.

words.definition: the appearance of being true or real
words.example: The novel's historical detail lends it great verisimilitude.

words.definition: the language or dialect spoken by ordinary people in a region
words.example: The poet wrote in the local vernacular rather than in formal English.

words.definition: remaining as a diminished remnant of something that was once functional
words.example: The appendix is often described as a vestigial organ.

words.definition: experienced through the feelings or actions of another person
words.example: She felt vicarious excitement watching her sister accept the award.

words.definition: filled with bitter criticism or malice
words.example: The critic's vitriolic review devastated the young playwright.

words.definition: vehement or clamorous in expressing one's views
words.example: A vociferous group of protesters gathered outside the courthouse.

words.definition: having or showing a feeling of vague or regretful longing
words.example: He gave a wistful glance at his childhood home as they drove past.

words.definition: the defining spirit or mood of a particular period in history
words.example: The film perfectly captured the zeitgeist of the 1960s.

words.definition: concerned with beauty or the appreciation of beauty
words.example: The architect prioritised aesthetic harmony over pure function.

words.definition: a story, poem, or picture that can be interpreted to reveal a hidden meaning
words.example: Animal Farm is a famous allegory of the Russian Revolution.

words.definition: the highest point in the development of something; elevation to divine status
words.example: The final symphony is the apotheosis of his career.

words.definition: a very typical example of a certain person or thing; an original model
words.example: The wise old mentor is a common archetype in mythology.

words.definition: a self-taught person
words.example: Largely an autodidact, she taught herself six languages from library books.

words.definition: a modulation or inflection in the rhythm and sound of language
words.example: The preacher's rising cadence held the congregation spellbound.

words.definition: accepted as a standard or authoritative part of a body of work
words.example: The novel is now considered part of the canonical literature of the period.

words.definition: the process of releasing strong or repressed emotions
words.example: Watching the tragedy provided a strange sense of catharsis.

words.definition: existing only as an illusion; wildly fanciful
words.example: His chimerical business plan collapsed within a month.

words.definition: a method of argument based on examining and reconciling contradictory ideas
words.example: The philosophy course centres on Hegelian dialectic.

words.definition: a sudden and striking realisation
words.example: She had an epiphany about her career while walking home.

words.definition: an idealising or excessively flattering biography
words.example: The documentary was criticised as hagiography rather than honest history.

words.definition: something reused or altered but still bearing visible traces of its earlier form
words.example: The city itself is a palimpsest of Roman, medieval, and modern architecture.

words.definition: an artistic work that imitates the style of another work, artist, or period
words.example: The film is a loving pastiche of 1980s adventure movies.

words.definition: excessively proud of oneself; boastful
words.example: The vainglorious general took credit for a victory he barely influenced.

words.definition: sharp and forthright in tone or wit
words.example: Her acerbic wit made her reviews feared across the theatre world.

words.definition: brisk and cheerful readiness
words.example: He accepted the challenge with surprising alacrity.

words.definition: something or someone that one vehemently dislikes
words.example: Censorship was anathema to the young publisher.

words.definition: ridiculously old-fashioned; belonging to a time before the biblical flood
words.example: He clung to antediluvian ideas about how novels should be written.

words.definition: a concise, witty statement of a general truth
words.example: Wilde's plays sparkle with quotable aphorisms.

words.definition: self-confidence and composure in a demanding situation
words.example: She handled the hostile interview with remarkable aplomb.

words.definition: the abandonment of a religious or political belief
words.example: His public apostasy from the party shocked former colleagues.

words.definition: approval or praise
words.example: The debut novel won the approbation of critics and readers alike.

words.definition: understood by few; mysterious or secret
words.example: The manuscript was written in an arcane legal shorthand.

words.definition: characterised by severe self-discipline and abstention from indulgence
words.example: The poet lived an ascetic life in a single bare room.

words.definition: harshness of tone or manner
words.example: 'You are late again,' she said with some asperity.

words.definition: showing great care, attention, and persistence
words.example: His assiduous editing removed every trace of cliché.

words.definition: relating to something ancient or ancestral, especially instincts
words.example: The storm awoke an atavistic fear in the sailors.

words.definition: kind and friendly toward a younger person, like an uncle
words.example: The presenter's avuncular manner reassured nervous contestants.

words.definition: blissfully happy; showing saintly serenity
words.example: A beatific smile spread across the child's sleeping face.

words.definition: a huge or monstrous creature; an enormous organisation
words.example: The corner shop struggled against the retail behemoths.

words.definition: demonstrating aggression and willingness to fight
words.example: The dictator's bellicose speeches alarmed neighbouring states.

words.definition: a novel dealing with a protagonist's formative years and moral education
words.example: Jane Eyre is a classic bildungsroman charting its heroine's growth.

words.definition: flattery intended to persuade or coax
words.example: No blandishments could persuade her to sell the family house.

words.definition: relating to the pleasant aspects of country life
words.example: The poem paints a bucolic scene of shepherds and meadows.

words.definition: a harsh, discordant mixture of sounds
words.example: A cacophony of car horns filled the gridlocked street.

words.definition: the making of false and defamatory statements about someone
words.example: The politician sued the paper for calumny.

words.definition: to reprimand someone severely
words.example: The coach castigated the defence for their sloppy marking.

words.definition: the use of trickery to achieve a political, financial, or legal purpose
words.example: The election was marred by legal chicanery on both sides.

words.definition: the use of many words where fewer would do, especially to be evasive
words.example: His answer was a masterpiece of circumlocution that said nothing.

words.definition: a concluding section that rounds off a piece of music or literature
words.example: The final chapter serves as a quiet coda to the novel's drama.

words.definition: clear, logical, and convincing
words.example: She made a cogent case for reforming the syllabus.

words.definition: naturally accompanying or associated
words.example: Fame brought its concomitant loss of privacy.

words.definition: feeling or expressing remorse
words.example: The contrite apology came too late to mend the friendship.

words.definition: physically bulky; fat
words.example: Dickens describes the corpulent alderman with obvious relish.

words.definition: flashing or sparkling; brilliantly witty or severe
words.example: The play's coruscating dialogue delighted the first-night audience.

words.definition: relating to twilight; active at dusk
words.example: Bats began their crepuscular hunt as the light failed.

words.definition: lacking a plan, purpose, or enthusiasm
words.example: They made desultory conversation while waiting for the train.

words.definition: a forceful and bitter verbal attack
words.example: The columnist launched into a diatribe against modern architecture.

words.definition: a person who cultivates an interest without real commitment or knowledge
words.example: Serious scholars dismissed him as a wealthy dilettante.

words.definition: a lament for the dead; a mournful song or piece
words.example: The bagpipes played a slow dirge as the procession passed.

words.definition: to persuade someone that a belief is mistaken
words.example: She quickly disabused him of the idea that the work would be easy.

words.definition: to conceal one's true motives or feelings
words.example: He dissembled his disappointment behind a gracious smile.

words.definition: indulging in sensual pleasures without restraint
words.example: The heir squandered the estate on a dissolute life abroad.

words.definition: comic verse of clumsy or irregular construction; badly written poetry
words.example: The greeting card's doggerel made the poet wince.

words.definition: feeling or expressing great sorrow
words.example: The dolorous tolling of the bell announced the news.

words.definition: irritable and bad-tempered, as if suffering from indigestion
words.example: The dyspeptic editor rejected manuscripts with withering notes.

words.definition: shining brightly; radiant
words.example: The effulgent moon silvered the entire bay.

words.definition: the action or means of going out or leaving a place
words.example: The fire regulations require two means of egress from the hall.

words.definition: the omission of a sound, syllable, or passage; a merging of things
words.example: The film's elision of the novel's middle chapters angered purists.

words.definition: a speech or piece of writing that praises someone highly
words.example: The retiring professor received encomium after encomium at the dinner.

words.definition: a pithy saying or short witty poem
words.example: He closed each chapter with a neat epigram.

words.definition: relating to the writing of letters; told through letters
words.example: The epistolary novel unfolds through decades of correspondence.

words.definition: giving one's name to something, such as a title character
words.example: The band's eponymous debut album topped the charts.

words.definition: being an inferior substitute or imitation
words.example: The café served an ersatz coffee brewed from chicory.

words.definition: to deliberately avoid or abstain from
words.example: The author eschews adverbs in favour of strong verbs.

words.definition: quickly fading or disappearing
words.example: The poem captures the evanescent beauty of cherry blossom.

words.definition: to criticise severely
words.example: Reviewers excoriated the sequel as a cynical cash-in.

words.definition: a critical explanation or interpretation of a text
words.example: Her exegesis of the sonnet ran to twenty pages.

words.definition: pressing and demanding urgent attention
words.example: Exigent circumstances forced the editor to cut the piece at midnight.

words.definition: to atone for guilt or wrongdoing
words.example: The narrator spends the novel trying to expiate a childhood betrayal.

words.definition: spoken or done without preparation
words.example: Her extemporaneous toast was funnier than any prepared speech.

words.definition: silly and pointless
words.example: The interviewer's fatuous questions wasted everyone's time.

words.definition: feverish; characterised by nervous excitement
words.example: Rumours spread quickly in the febrile atmosphere before the vote.

words.definition: to protest loudly and bitterly
words.example: The colonel fulminated against the decision in the letters page.

words.definition: to deny or contradict
words.example: There is no gainsaying her influence on modern poetry.

words.definition: an organised whole perceived as more than the sum of its parts
words.example: The exhibition's gestalt mattered more than any single painting.

words.definition: extremely light, thin, and delicate
words.example: Gossamer threads of mist hung over the river at dawn.

words.definition: pompous or extravagant in language or style
words.example: His grandiloquent acceptance speech drew stifled laughter.

words.definition: dignity, seriousness, and solemnity of manner
words.example: The veteran actor brought real gravitas to the small role.

words.definition: denoting an idyllically happy and peaceful period in the past
words.example: She wrote longingly of the halcyon summers of her childhood.

words.definition: a lengthy and aggressive speech
words.example: The manager delivered a half-time harangue that left ears ringing.

words.definition: haughtiness of manner; disdainful pride
words.example: The duchess regarded the newcomers with icy hauteur.

words.definition: not conforming to accepted standards or beliefs
words.example: His heterodox reading of the play divided the seminar.

words.definition: overly theatrical or melodramatic
words.example: Her histrionic sobbing convinced no one on the jury.

words.definition: tending to exhort; strongly urging a course of action
words.example: The pamphlet's hortatory tone urged readers to act at once.

words.definition: deserving or causing public disgrace
words.example: The campaign ended in ignominious retreat.

words.definition: an extremely confused or embarrassing situation
words.example: The leaked emails plunged the ministry into a fresh imbroglio.

words.definition: having little or no money
words.example: The impecunious student lived on toast and library heating.

words.definition: persistent to the point of annoyance in making requests
words.example: The importunate salesman rang for the fifth time that week.

words.definition: a spoken curse
words.example: He muttered imprecations at the printer as it jammed again.

words.definition: tending to obstruct or harm; hostile
words.example: The critic argued that fame is inimical to serious writing.

words.definition: immoral or grossly unfair behaviour
words.example: The novel exposes the iniquities of the workhouse system.

words.definition: impossible to overcome
words.example: The funding gap proved an insuperable obstacle to the expedition.

words.definition: accustomed to something unpleasant through prolonged exposure
words.example: Years on the night desk left her inured to grim headlines.

words.definition: having a long-established and unlikely-to-change habit
words.example: An inveterate gambler, he could not pass a betting shop.

words.definition: naive and simplistic; dull and lacking substance
words.example: The professor dismissed the essay's argument as jejune.

words.definition: a long, mournful complaint or lamentation
words.example: His column became a weekly jeremiad about declining standards.

words.definition: tearful or inclined to weep; inducing tears
words.example: The film's lachrymose finale left the audience sniffling.

words.definition: glowing or flickering with a soft radiance
words.example: Lambent firelight played across the cottage walls.

words.definition: elegant and precise, as if engraved in stone
words.example: Her lapidary prose wastes not a single word.

words.definition: a recurrent theme associated with a person or idea in a work
words.example: Exile is the leitmotif running through all her fiction.

words.definition: disregarding accepted rules, especially in sexual conduct
words.example: The court's licentious excesses scandalised the clergy.

words.definition: completely clear and transparent; effortlessly lucid
words.example: She admired the limpid clarity of his translation.

words.definition: relating to the shore of a sea or lake
words.example: The littoral zone teems with crabs and anemones at low tide.

words.definition: a particular word, phrase, or style of expression
words.example: The lawyer's careful locutions revealed nothing of his client's plans.

words.definition: a tedious passage in a book or performance
words.example: Despite occasional longueurs, the opera rewards patience.

words.definition: disreputable in a rakish or appealing way
words.example: The novel is set in the louche nightclubs of interwar Berlin.

words.definition: self-pityingly or tearfully sentimental
words.example: After midnight the reunion turned maudlin, full of old regrets.

words.definition: sentimental in a sickly or feeble way
words.example: The obituary avoided the mawkish tributes the star would have hated.

words.definition: having a sharply critical or biting quality
words.example: Her mordant humour spared neither friend nor foe.

words.definition: at the point of death; in terminal decline
words.example: The critic pronounced the genre moribund — a week before its revival.

words.definition: larger or more generous than is usual or necessary
words.example: A munificent bequest saved the museum from closure.

words.definition: the lowest point in fortunes or circumstances
words.example: The scandal marked the nadir of his political career.

words.definition: having a strong religious or spiritual quality; awe-inspiring
words.example: The empty cathedral had a numinous stillness at dusk.

words.definition: stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action
words.example: The landlord remained obdurate despite every appeal.

words.definition: the complete body of an artist's or writer's work
words.example: The retrospective surveys the photographer's entire oeuvre.

words.definition: a solution or remedy for all difficulties
words.example: Technology is no panacea for the challenges facing schools.

words.definition: a song or expression of praise and triumph
words.example: The review was less criticism than a paean to the director.

words.definition: extremely unwilling to spend money or use resources
words.example: The parsimonious trustee questioned every penny of the budget.

words.definition: translucently clear; easily understood
words.example: Her pellucid prose makes difficult philosophy feel simple.

words.definition: extremely poor; unwilling to spend money
words.example: The penurious scholar copied books he could not afford to buy.

words.definition: a long, meandering journey
words.example: His peregrinations took him from Lisbon to Vladivostok.

words.definition: deceitful and untrustworthy
words.example: The perfidious courtier betrayed his king for a title.

words.definition: the concluding, often rousing, part of a speech
words.example: The barrister's peroration moved several jurors to tears.

words.definition: having a calm, unemotional disposition
words.example: The phlegmatic pilot announced the engine failure as if reading a shopping list.

words.definition: loud, resonant, and often melancholy in sound
words.example: The plangent notes of the cello filled the empty church.

words.definition: a remark used too often to be interesting or thoughtful
words.example: The minister offered platitudes instead of policies.

words.definition: an expression of praise or approval
words.example: The restoration won plaudits from heritage groups.

words.definition: an abundance; the condition of being full
words.example: The harvest festival celebrated the season's plenitude.

words.definition: a person of wide-ranging knowledge or learning
words.example: A true polymath, she published on astronomy, music, and law.

words.definition: a sign or warning that something momentous is likely to happen
words.example: The sudden silence of the birds felt like a portent.

words.definition: beyond what is normal or natural
words.example: The goalkeeper showed preternatural calm in the shoot-out.

words.definition: complete honesty and integrity
words.example: The auditor's probity was beyond question.

words.definition: using too many words; tediously lengthy
words.example: The prolix report buried its one useful finding in eighty pages.

words.definition: to promote or make widely known; to put a law into effect
words.example: The decree was promulgated throughout the empire.

words.definition: to win or regain the favour of by doing something pleasing
words.example: Ancient sailors made offerings to propitiate the sea gods.

words.definition: giving a good chance of success; favourable
words.example: With markets booming, the timing for the launch was propitious.

words.definition: to forbid, especially by law
words.example: The new charter proscribed duelling within the city walls.

words.definition: tending to change frequently; versatile
words.example: The protean actor was unrecognisable from one role to the next.

words.definition: childishly silly and immature
words.example: The critics dismissed the sketch as puerile humour.

words.definition: eager or quick to argue or fight
words.example: The pugnacious columnist picked a fight with half of Fleet Street.

words.definition: physical beauty
words.example: The poem praises the pulchritude of its unnamed subject.

words.definition: showing great attention to detail or correct behaviour
words.example: He was punctilious about answering every letter within a day.

words.definition: showing a lack of courage; timid
words.example: The pusillanimous board caved at the first sign of protest.

words.definition: in a state of inactivity or dormancy
words.example: The volcano has remained quiescent for two centuries.

words.definition: occurring every day; ordinary or mundane
words.example: Her diary transforms quotidian errands into small comedies.

words.definition: a person skilled at telling stories in an amusing way
words.example: A born raconteur, he could stretch a train delay into an epic.

words.definition: aggressively greedy or grasping
words.example: Rapacious landlords doubled rents overnight.

words.definition: understood by only a select group; esoteric; of thin air
words.example: She moved in the rarefied world of rare-book auctions.

words.definition: unattractive and objectionable; repellent
words.example: Even admirers found the composer's late work rebarbative.

words.definition: the recurrence of an undesirable condition after a dormant period
words.example: Historians traced the recrudescence of old rivalries after the treaty lapsed.

words.definition: formidable, especially as an opponent; worthy of respect
words.example: She faced the redoubtable champion without flinching.

words.definition: shining very brightly
words.example: The refulgent dome could be seen from every quarter of the city.

words.definition: to make a forcefully reproachful protest
words.example: The captain remonstrated with the referee to no avail.

words.definition: becoming active or popular again
words.example: The renascent vinyl industry outsold CDs for the first time in decades.

words.definition: an unprincipled, disreputable person
words.example: The old reprobate charmed his way back into every club that had expelled him.

words.definition: unable to keep still or silent; restless under restriction
words.example: The restive crowd began chanting before the delayed kick-off.

words.definition: provoking laughter, especially through absurdity
words.example: The villain's risible disguise fooled no one in the audience.

words.definition: boastful or inflated talk
words.example: His after-dinner speech was pure rodomontade.

words.definition: rose-coloured; unduly optimistic
words.example: The prospectus painted a roseate picture of the company's future.

words.definition: having a healthy reddish complexion

words.definition: to think deeply about something

words.definition: having keen judgement; wise

words.definition: health-giving; pleasant and respectable

words.definition: optimistic, especially in a difficult situation
words.example: She remained sanguine about the book's prospects despite early rejections.

words.definition: gloomy and brooding in temperament or appearance
words.example: The saturnine detective rarely smiled, even at a solved case.

words.definition: pleasure derived from another person's misfortune

words.definition: a tiny trace or amount

words.definition: making scandalous and defamatory claims

words.definition: gloomy and suggestive of the grave

words.definition: an image or representation, often an unsatisfactory imitation

words.definition: a position requiring little work but giving status or profit

words.definition: a grammatical mistake; a breach of etiquette

words.definition: sleepy; inducing drowsiness

words.definition: tending to induce sleep; tediously boring
words.example: The lecturer's soporific drone defeated even the front row.

words.definition: false or fake, though seeming plausible

words.definition: extremely loud and powerful in sound

words.definition: enduring pain or hardship without complaint

words.definition: extremely dark, as of the underworld river Styx

words.definition: to channel an impulse into a more socially acceptable activity

words.definition: deceit used to achieve one's goal

words.definition: behaving as though one is superior to others

words.definition: outdated or retired because of age

words.definition: lying face upwards; failing to act through laziness or weakness

words.definition: an excessive amount of something

words.definition: a whispering or rustling sound

words.definition: a person devoted to luxury and pleasure
words.example: A confirmed sybarite, he never travelled without silk sheets.

words.definition: a person who flatters the powerful for personal gain

words.definition: understood or implied without being stated

words.definition: reserved and saying little

words.definition: expressing a controversial opinion with a hidden purpose; biased

words.definition: dark and shadowy
words.example: They descended into the tenebrous crypt with a single lantern.

words.definition: evasive or ambiguous statements; abandonment of a cause

words.definition: showing nervousness or a lack of confidence

words.definition: to speak badly of or tell lies about someone

words.definition: to change in form, nature, or substance

words.definition: vigorously incisive and effective

words.definition: swollen; pompously overblown in language

words.definition: depraved or wicked behaviour

words.definition: not restricted or hampered

words.definition: excessively devoted to one's wife

words.definition: to waver between different opinions or actions

words.definition: offering no stimulation or challenge; dull

words.definition: showing different colours; marked by variety

words.definition: open to bribery; corruptly mercenary

words.definition: to regard with great respect; to revere

words.definition: excessive and often meaningless use of words
words.example: Strip away the verbiage and the contract says very little.

words.definition: green with grass or lush vegetation
words.example: The verdant valley stretched below the monastery.

words.definition: causing dizziness; extremely high or steep

words.definition: bitter and abusive in language

words.definition: a devoted follower or advocate
words.example: A lifelong votary of Bach, she played the cello suites daily.

words.definition: a large, confused number or mass of things

words.definition: attractive or appealing in a fresh, innocent way

words.definition: a soft, gentle breeze

words.definition: the highest point in the development of something

words.definition: a thing regarded as too unimportant to bother with; a trifle

words.definition: a trite statement intended to soothe or placate

words.definition: to make petty or unnecessary objections

words.definition: cautiously reluctant to do something

words.definition: greed for money or possessions

words.definition: relating to ordinary, everyday language of the people

words.definition: listlessness arising from boredom or lack of purpose

words.definition: coolness or reserve between people

words.definition: lacking flavour, vigour, or interest

words.definition: the act of renouncing or rejecting something; self-denial
words.example: Her life of quiet abnegation funded her nephew's education.

words.definition: to repeal or do away with a law or agreement

words.definition: to outline or foreshadow in a vague way
words.example: The opening chapter adumbrates the tragedy to come.

words.definition: to increase the power or reputation of, often unjustifiably

words.definition: the scope or extent of something

words.definition: a literary assistant who writes from dictation

words.definition: unlikely to cause offence; blandly inoffensive

words.definition: a deep-seated feeling of dislike

words.definition: apt in the circumstances; strikingly appropriate

words.definition: an ambitious person who has recently risen in status

words.definition: a workshop or studio of an artist or designer

words.definition: respected and impressive; majestic

words.definition: the protection or support of a person or organisation

words.definition: humorous or witty conversation

words.definition: one's sphere of operations or area of expertise

words.definition: threatening harm; menacing

words.definition: to charm, sometimes deceptively; to pass time pleasantly

words.definition: in a state of ignorance, typically owing to lack of opportunity

words.definition: excessively fond of drinking alcohol

words.definition: small and elegant

words.definition: showing a casual and cheerful indifference

words.definition: cheerful friendliness and good nature

words.definition: to remove material considered improper from a text

words.definition: boastful or arrogant behaviour

words.definition: a member of a gang that robs travellers

words.definition: irritatingly self-assertive

words.definition: a secret group of plotters or intriguers

words.definition: prestige or distinction attached to something

words.definition: inexperienced and immature

words.definition: tending to find fault or raise petty objections

words.definition: of the same age or period

words.definition: roomy and comfortable
words.example: The commodious study held ten thousand books.

words.definition: containing much information concisely

words.definition: willing to please others; obliging

words.definition: to conduct oneself; to behave in a particular manner

words.definition: a series of interconnected things or events

words.definition: (of punishment) fitting and deserved

words.definition: to chat; to fabricate imaginary experiences believed to be true

words.definition: a minor dispute or embarrassing mishap

words.definition: friendly, lively, and enjoyable

words.definition: behaving in a playfully flirtatious way

words.definition: to care for and protect in an overindulgent way

words.definition: a small exclusive group with shared interests

words.definition: refined and polite, befitting a royal court

words.definition: contemptibly lacking in courage

words.definition: a statement of beliefs that guides one's actions

words.definition: a person or thing that is the centre of attention

words.definition: a casual romantic or brief involvement with something

words.definition: confident, stylish, and charming

words.definition: to speak rhetorically or with theatrical passion

words.definition: the act of throwing someone out of a window; a swift removal from power

words.definition: to raise objections or show reluctance

words.definition: an inhabitant or frequenter of a particular place

words.definition: to uproot someone from their natural environment

words.definition: dried out; lacking vitality or interest

words.definition: waste or debris; accumulated fragments

words.definition: a lack of harmony; tension between conflicting elements

words.definition: brave and persistent

words.definition: insolent or impertinent boldness

words.definition: energy, style, and enthusiasm

words.definition: attempting to soothe or calm; softening

words.definition: relating to the highest heaven; celestial

words.definition: a burden or impediment

words.definition: to drain of energy or vitality

words.definition: to reveal the presence of a quality or feeling

words.definition: extremely bad or unpleasant

words.definition: very small in amount; scanty

words.definition: to speak or write at length on a subject

words.definition: to express strong disagreement or protest
words.example: She expostulated in vain against the committee's choice.

words.definition: to erase or remove completely

words.definition: to root out and destroy completely

words.definition: an employee who does all kinds of work

words.definition: a confused mixture

words.definition: formal loyalty, originally sworn to a lord

words.definition: lacking initiative or strength of character; irresponsible

words.definition: to adorn with chains or garlands of decoration

words.definition: irritable and quarrelsome; difficult to control

words.definition: excessively flattering; abundant

words.definition: to run or jump about playfully

words.definition: socially awkward or tactless

words.definition: relevant to a subject under consideration

words.definition: showy but flimsy or worthless

words.definition: to stare with sullen anger

words.definition: expressed in short, cryptic sayings

words.definition: lacking sense or initiative; foolish

words.definition: relating to the sense of taste

words.definition: a regular visitor to a place

words.definition: unfortunate; unlucky

words.definition: a person or thing that signals the approach of another

words.definition: unwilling to abandon old-fashioned ideas

words.definition: the remote areas beyond a coast or river; one's background knowledge

words.definition: greyish-white with age; old and trite

words.definition: a moralising lecture; a religious sermon

words.definition: the visual images and symbols used in a work or field

words.definition: a blissfully peaceful and picturesque period or scene

words.definition: not honourable in character or purpose

words.definition: to inspire or fill with a quality

words.definition: existing or operating within; inherent

words.definition: to enclose or confine against one's will

words.definition: not feeling or showing emotion

words.definition: arrogantly domineering

words.definition: official approval or sanction

words.definition: failing to provide for the future; wasteful

words.definition: to dispute the truth or honesty of
words.example: She resented any attempt to impugn her motives.

words.definition: silly and lacking sense or meaning

words.definition: glowing with heat; furious; brilliant

words.definition: out of place; not in harmony with the surroundings

words.definition: without physical form or substance

words.definition: persisting tirelessly

words.definition: impossible to remove or forget

words.definition: wanting to avoid activity or exertion; lazy

words.definition: impossible to stop or prevent

words.definition: an innocent or unsophisticated young woman, especially in drama

words.definition: impossible to understand or interpret
words.example: The examiner's inscrutable expression gave nothing away.

words.definition: lacking physical sensation or compassion
words.example: The critics called the demolition an act of insensate vandalism.

words.definition: hard to control or deal with; stubborn

words.definition: free from injury or violation; kept sacred

words.definition: likely to arouse resentment; unfairly discriminating

words.definition: easily made angry

words.definition: travelling from place to place for work

words.definition: a huge, overwhelming, unstoppable force

words.definition: a military group ruling after taking power by force

words.definition: art or objects considered tasteless but enjoyed ironically

words.definition: like a maze; intricately complicated

words.definition: a state of physical or mental weariness

words.definition: skilful deception; sleight of hand

words.definition: the vocabulary of a person, language, or subject

words.definition: a person who freely indulges in pleasures without morals

words.definition: to treat someone as a celebrity

words.definition: slim, supple, and graceful

words.definition: a tedious recital or repetitive series

words.definition: money, especially when gained dishonourably

words.definition: a person who inspires or influences others in a field

words.definition: cunning and unscrupulous, especially in politics

words.definition: a powerful whirlpool; a situation of confused turmoil

words.definition: clumsy or inept, especially socially

words.definition: a beggar; a member of a religious order living on alms

words.definition: a person's social environment

words.definition: the arrangement of scenery and stage properties; a setting

words.definition: massive, uniform, and slow to change

words.definition: having many varied parts or aspects

words.definition: short-sighted; lacking foresight

words.definition: just coming into existence and beginning to develop

words.definition: a newly coined word or expression

words.definition: an unbeatable rival; an agent of downfall

words.definition: a person new to a subject or activity

words.definition: to irritate or annoy
words.example: The interviewer's smirk clearly nettled the minister.

words.definition: having an extremely offensive smell; disagreeable
words.example: A noisome vapour rose from the abandoned tannery.

words.definition: so surprised and confused that one is unsure how to react
words.example: The question left the panel entirely nonplussed.

words.definition: a quack remedy; a pet scheme for improvement
words.example: The pamphlet peddled the usual economic nostrums.

words.definition: of no value or importance; useless
words.example: Without enforcement, the ban is nugatory.

words.definition: deferential respect; a bow or curtsy
words.example: The courtiers made deep obeisance as the empress passed.

words.definition: strong public criticism; disgrace from it
words.example: He endured years of obloquy before being vindicated.

words.definition: to remove a need or difficulty; to prevent
words.example: The new footbridge obviates the long detour to the lock.

words.definition: to block or close up; to obscure
words.example: Scaffolding occluded the cathedral's famous west front.

words.definition: widespread hatred or disgust
words.example: The betrayal earned him the lasting odium of his party.

words.definition: asserting authority in an annoyingly domineering way
words.example: An officious steward demanded to see everyone's lanyard twice.

words.definition: exaggeratedly complimentary; oily
words.example: The maître d's oleaginous flattery grated on the guests.

words.definition: to hold and state as one's opinion
words.example: The pundit opined that the era of the winger was over.

words.definition: resembling an oracle; wise but obscure and ambiguous
words.example: The old trainer's oracular sayings were quoted for decades.

words.definition: full and imposing in voice; pompous in style
words.example: The actor's orotund delivery suited the ancient role.

words.definition: to turn into bone; to become rigid and cease developing
words.example: Without new members, the committee's thinking ossified.

words.definition: serving no practical purpose; pointless
words.example: The final chapter feels otiose after the story's true climax.

words.definition: showing excessive confidence or pride
words.example: His overweening ambition alienated every ally he had.

words.definition: prolonged and tedious fuss or discussion
words.example: Renewing the permit involved no end of palaver.

words.definition: pale, especially from illness; feeble or insipid
words.example: A pallid sun struggled through the November mist.

words.definition: a complete and impressive array
words.example: The coronation deployed the full panoply of state.

words.definition: the collective revered figures of a field; all the gods of a people
words.example: She earned her place in the pantheon of great mountaineers.

words.definition: a person newly wealthy or influential but not yet accepted socially
words.example: The old families snubbed the railway parvenu and his gilded villa.

words.definition: the presence of something in only small or insufficient amounts
words.example: The trial collapsed for a paucity of reliable evidence.

words.definition: a small, relatively unimportant sin or fault
words.example: The biography dwells too long on the composer's peccadilloes.

words.definition: a teacher, especially a strict or pedantic one
words.example: The old pedagogue corrected the bishop's Latin at dinner.

words.definition: excessively concerned with minor details and rules
words.example: His pedantic corrections derailed every meeting.

words.definition: a partial shadow; an area of uncertainty at the edge of something
words.example: The case falls within the penumbra of the old statute.

words.definition: to walk or travel through, especially at leisure
words.example: They perambulated the cloisters discussing the sermon.

words.definition: a special benefit attached to a position
words.example: Use of the lodge was a perquisite of the wardenship.

words.definition: light, teasing banter
words.example: Beneath the persiflage, the rivals respected each other deeply.

words.definition: a compact body of people or things, originally troops
words.example: A phalanx of photographers blocked the courthouse steps.

words.definition: a bitter verbal attack or denunciation
words.example: The backbencher delivered a philippic against the budget.

words.definition: pleasantly sharp in taste; stimulating to the mind
words.example: The memoir offers piquant sketches of post-war Soho.

words.definition: irritation from wounded pride; (verb) to stimulate interest
words.example: He resigned in a fit of pique over the seating plan.

words.definition: sounding sad and mournful
words.example: A plaintive violin drifted from the upstairs window.

words.definition: attended by all members; complete in every respect
words.example: The treaty was signed at a plenary session of the congress.

words.definition: an excessive or overabundant amount
words.example: The report offered a plethora of statistics and no conclusions.

words.definition: an utter coward
words.example: The colonel denounced the deserters as poltroons.

words.definition: a word blending the sounds and meanings of two others; a large travelling bag
words.example: 'Brunch' is a portmanteau of breakfast and lunch.

words.definition: to talk at length in a foolish or inconsequential way
words.example: The tour guide prattled on while the group admired the view.

words.definition: to cause to happen suddenly or prematurely
words.example: The bank's collapse precipitated a general panic.

words.definition: having knowledge of events before they happen
words.example: Her prescient warning about the dam was ignored for years.

words.definition: to speak evasively to avoid telling the truth
words.example: Stop prevaricating and tell me who broke the window.

words.definition: a self-righteously moralistic person
words.example: Only a prig would object to one small firework.

words.definition: existing from the beginning of time; primeval
words.example: Life began in some warm primordial sea.

words.definition: recklessly extravagant or wasteful
words.example: The profligate duke mortgaged the estate for one summer's racing.

words.definition: nearness in place, time, or relationship
words.example: Friendship often grows from simple propinquity.

words.definition: the patterns of rhythm and sound in poetry
words.example: The seminar traced the prosody of the Anglo-Saxon epic.

words.definition: food or provisions, especially for animals
words.example: The barn was stacked with winter provender.

words.definition: having great power or influence
words.example: The ballad praises the puissant lords of the border.

words.definition: an expert who gives opinions publicly
words.example: The pundits unanimously predicted the wrong winner.

words.definition: to steal, often something of small value
words.example: Someone had purloined the last biscuit from the tin.

words.definition: generally considered or reputed to be
words.example: The putative author of the letters remains disputed.

words.definition: a soft boggy area; an awkward, inescapable situation
words.example: The inquiry sank into a legal quagmire.

words.definition: an uneasy feeling of doubt about one's conduct
words.example: She had no qualms about reporting the fraud.

words.definition: a state of stillness and calm
words.example: He prized the quietude of the college library at dawn.

words.definition: a traitor who collaborates with an occupying enemy
words.example: History remembers the quislings with unsparing contempt.

words.definition: clothing, especially fine garments
words.example: The procession glittered with ceremonial raiment.

words.definition: dashingly stylish in a slightly disreputable way
words.example: He wore his hat at a rakish angle.

words.definition: a resumption of friendly relations
words.example: The summit produced a cautious rapprochement between the rivals.

words.definition: exact, logical reasoning
words.example: The detective solved the case by pure ratiocination.

words.definition: to withdraw a former belief or statement publicly
words.example: Threatened with exile, the philosopher refused to recant.

words.definition: rare, exotic, or obscure; chosen with care
words.example: The menu's recherché ingredients needed footnotes.

words.definition: the tendency of a convicted criminal to reoffend
words.example: The mentoring scheme halved recidivism among young offenders.

words.definition: lying down
words.example: The tomb bears a recumbent effigy of the knight.

words.definition: stubborn or unmanageable; resistant to treatment
words.example: The refractory mule refused every path but its own.

words.definition: to entertain lavishly with stories or food
words.example: He regaled the table with tales of the delta blues.

words.definition: lacking care or attention to duty
words.example: I would be remiss not to thank our volunteers.

words.definition: money paid for work or a service
words.example: The role's remuneration hardly matched its demands.

words.definition: quick, witty conversation or replies
words.example: Their repartee kept the whole dinner table laughing.

words.definition: to feel or express discontent; to fret
words.example: She refused to repine over chances long gone.

words.definition: to reprimand or express disapproval gently
words.example: The abbot reproved the novice with a single glance.

words.definition: to return a favour or feeling; to repay
words.example: His devotion, alas, was never requited.

words.definition: to revoke or cancel a law or agreement
words.example: The board rescinded the ban after the appeal.

words.definition: attractive and impressive through being richly colourful
words.example: The regiment marched past resplendent in scarlet.

words.definition: a person who returns, especially supposedly from the dead
words.example: The village tale tells of a revenant who walks the causeway.

words.definition: referring to sexual matters in an amusingly rude way
words.example: The tavern rang with ribald songs till closing.

words.definition: a fixed grimace or grin
words.example: His face froze in a rictus of forced jollity.

words.definition: relating to the banks of a river
words.example: Otters have returned to the riparian meadows.

words.definition: a raised platform for speaking or conducting
words.example: The conductor mounted the rostrum to warm applause.

words.definition: a noisy disturbance or quarrel
words.example: There'll be ructions when the changes are announced.

words.definition: expressing regret, often with wry humour
words.example: He gave a rueful smile at the missed open goal.

words.definition: regarded as too important to be interfered with
words.example: Sunday lunch was sacrosanct in that household.

words.definition: producing good effects, though often unwelcome
words.example: The near-miss was a salutary lesson in checking the ropes.

words.definition: composure or coolness under strain
words.example: With perfect sang-froid, she corrected the minister's figures.

words.definition: wise; attempting to appear wise
words.example: The owl's sapient stare belies a modest intellect.

words.definition: a descendant of a notable family; a plant shoot for grafting
words.example: The scion of the banking dynasty chose to teach instead.

words.definition: rigid and unable to adapt; hardened
words.example: Reformers blamed the sclerotic planning system for the shortage.

words.definition: conduct or speech inciting rebellion against authority
words.example: The printer was gaoled for sedition over the pamphlet.

words.definition: given to pompous moralising
words.example: The sententious narrator lectures the reader at every turn.

words.definition: angelically blissful or serene
words.example: The sleeping infant wore a seraphic expression.

words.definition: relating to the distant stars
words.example: The observatory keeps sidereal time for its measurements.

words.definition: to smile in an affectedly coy manner
words.example: The courtiers simpered at the prince's feeble joke.

words.definition: underhand or dishonest behaviour
words.example: The transfer was surrounded by rumours of skulduggery.

words.definition: a person's nickname
words.example: He earned the sobriquet 'The Professor' for his tactical brain.

words.definition: a temporary stay in a place
words.example: Her Roman sojourn stretched from a month to a decade.

words.definition: showing attentive care and concern
words.example: The solicitous host kept refilling every glass.

words.definition: deep, full, and imposing in sound
words.example: The actor's sonorous voice filled the amphitheatre unamplified.

words.definition: marked by strict self-discipline and lack of comfort
words.example: The training camp's spartan dormitories shocked the recruits.

words.definition: bad temper; spite
words.example: He vented his spleen on the hapless referee.

words.definition: bad-tempered and spiteful
words.example: The splenetic columnist spared no one, least of all himself.

words.definition: sedate, respectable, and unadventurous
words.example: The staid old firm surprised everyone with its neon rebrand.

words.definition: loyal, reliable, and hard-working
words.example: Stalwart volunteers kept the food bank open through the freeze.

words.definition: a plan or scheme to outwit an opponent
words.example: The feigned retreat was a classic battlefield stratagem.

words.definition: a sternly critical remark; a restriction
words.example: The strictures of the censor shaped a generation of film-makers.

words.definition: to cause to lose enthusiasm, especially through tedium
words.example: Endless form-filling stultified the ward's best nurses.

words.definition: of such excellence or beauty as to inspire awe
words.example: From the ridge, the dawn view was simply sublime.

words.definition: assistance in time of hardship
words.example: The abbey offered succour to travellers caught by the snow.

words.definition: the right of one state to partly control another
words.example: The border princes lived under the empire's suzerainty.

words.definition: slender and elegant
words.example: The svelte new terminal replaced a concrete monolith.

words.definition: engaging in daring, romantic adventures with bravado
words.example: The team's swashbuckling style thrilled the neutrals.

words.definition: relating to woods; wooded
words.example: The path wound through a sylvan valley of old oaks.

words.definition: a conference to discuss a particular academic subject
words.example: She presented her findings at the Vienna symposium.

words.definition: a figure of speech in which a part stands for the whole
words.example: 'All hands on deck' is a synecdoche — the hands stand for sailors.

words.definition: excessive boldness; audacity
words.example: He had the temerity to correct the examiner's arithmetic.

words.definition: characterised by strong, turbulent emotion; stormy
words.example: Their tempestuous partnership produced three great albums.

words.definition: a principle or belief held as true by a group
words.example: Fair play is a central tenet of the club's academy.

words.definition: very weak or slight; insubstantial
words.example: The link between the two cases was tenuous at best.

words.definition: holding a title without real authority; relating to a title
words.example: The titular head of state wields no real power.

words.definition: to promote or praise energetically
words.example: The pundits touted the teenager as the next great hope.

words.definition: easy to control, manage, or influence
words.example: The problem proved more tractable once split into stages.

words.definition: to transform into something more beautiful or elevated
words.example: Snow transfigured the scrapyard into a field of soft hills.

words.definition: a cause of great trouble or suffering
words.example: The memoir recounts the trials and tribulations of exile.

words.definition: a cave dweller; a person deliberately ignorant or old-fashioned
words.example: He cheerfully calls himself a troglodyte for refusing a smartphone.

words.definition: a statement so obviously true it is barely worth saying
words.example: That practice improves performance is a truism of coaching.

words.definition: protection of or authority over someone; instruction
words.example: Under her tutelage, the choir won its first national title.

words.definition: a beginner or novice
words.example: For a tyro, she handled the yacht remarkably well.

words.definition: complete and unqualified; pure
words.example: The homecoming was a moment of unalloyed joy.

words.definition: not right or reasonable; unreasonably excessive
words.example: The delay in paying the victims was unconscionable.

words.definition: to move with a smooth wave-like motion
words.example: Wheat undulated across the hillside in the wind.

words.definition: a soft ointment or salve
words.example: The apothecary sold unguents for every imaginable ache.

words.definition: not able to be defended against attack or objection
words.example: After the vote, the chairman's position became untenable.

words.definition: an unpredictable change or action
words.example: Farmers live at the mercy of the weather's vagaries.

words.definition: serving as a farewell
words.example: His valedictory lecture filled the great hall twice over.

words.definition: the leading position in a movement; the front of an army
words.example: The studio was in the vanguard of hand-drawn animation's revival.

words.definition: praised or boasted about, often more than deserved
words.example: The team's vaunted defence conceded four in the first half.

words.definition: a comprehensive world view or philosophy of life
words.example: The trilogy articulates the author's entire weltanschauung.
